name: Iron Ore & Coking Coal Suppliers
description: Represents the upstream raw material suppliers — both domestic iron ore miners (NMDC, Tata Steel Mining) and international coking coal exporters (Australia, Mozambique). These suppliers control the key cost inputs for steel production.
objectives: Maximise revenue from raw material sales
Exploit supply-demand imbalances to improve pricing power
Maintain long-term supply relationships with major steel producers
Secure mining leases and expand production capacity
constraints: Export duties on iron ore (India imposes 30% on lumps, 0% on pellets)
Logistics bottlenecks — rail and port capacity limits
Global commodity price cycles — cannot control global benchmarks
Long-term contracts lock in pricing for 6-12 months

supply_dynamics.iron_ore_supply_elasticity: Low in short run (6-12 months to ramp up)
supply_dynamics.coking_coal_supply_concentration: Top 4 Australian miners control 60% of seaborne trade
supply_dynamics.logistics_bottleneck: Indian railways handles 65% of iron ore movement
supply_dynamics.monsoon_impact: Mining output drops 15-20% during Jun-Sep monsoon

pricing_power_triggers.demand_surge: Steel capacity expansion → immediate ore price increase
pricing_power_triggers.supply_disruption: Cyclone, mine accident, export ban → price spike
pricing_power_triggers.china_factor: Chinese demand changes swing global prices ±20%
pricing_power_triggers.trade_diversion: If steel exports blocked from one market, domestic demand for ore may shift
